topic: new winter menu — roasted root vegetables and game

turns.content: I'm working on a new dish for the winter menu and I want your opinion. Venison loin with a celery root purée and roasted sunchokes. What do you think?

That's a strong combination. Venison loin is lean and intensely flavoured, celery root purée gives you a silky, earthy base, and sunchokes add a nutty sweetness that contrasts nicely. Classic winter plate.

A few questions to sharpen the concept: how are you cooking the venison? And what's your sauce situation -- are you going jus-based, or something with more acidity?

seared and finished in the oven, rested properly. I want it medium rare — anything more and it's leather. For sauce I'm thinking a red wine jus with juniper and a touch of dark chocolate. Not sweet, just that bitterness to deepen it

Juniper and dark chocolate in the jus is excellent. The juniper is a natural pairing with venison -- it echoes that wild, gamey quality -- and the chocolate adds depth without sweetness if you keep it to a very small amount. Maybe 5-10 grams of high-percentage dark chocolate per litre of jus. Just enough that you can't identify it but you'd miss it if it weren't there.

For the sear: get that pan screaming hot and use a neutral oil with a high smoke point. Venison loin is so lean that you need an aggressive sear to get the crust before the interior overcooks. Baste with butter, thyme, and garlic in the last minute.

How are you handling the sunchokes? Roasted whole, or cut?

halved, roasted at high heat with thyme and brown butter. I want them caramelized, almost crispy on the cut side but creamy inside. The problem is they oxidize so fast — by the time I've cut ten of them the first ones are already turning grey

The oxidation is the eternal sunchoke problem. A few approaches:

- **Acidulated water** -- keep a bowl of water with lemon juice next to your board. Drop each sunchoke half in as you cut. Drain and pat dry before roasting. The acid prevents browning without affecting the final flavour since it cooks off.
- **Cut and roast immediately in small batches** during service rather than cutting them all during prep. More work during service but they look pristine.
- **Embrace it slightly** -- a little oxidation disappears once they caramelize. If you're roasting them hard enough, the browning from the Maillard reaction masks any discolouration.

Honestly, for a fine dining plate, the acidulated water method is the safest. You want that clean ivory colour against the dark venison.

acidulated water, oui, I do that already but I feel like I lose some flavour. Maybe I'm being paranoid. The small batch approach during service is tempting but on a busy night that's one more thing to manage on the station. I think I'll prep them in the water and then get a hard sear to compensate

The flavour loss from a brief soak in acidulated water is minimal -- you'd need them in there for hours before it really matters. If you're cutting and soaking within 30 minutes of service and then drying them well before they hit the pan, the difference is negligible. And you're right, the hard sear will bring all that flavour back through caramelization.

Now let's talk about the plate composition. You've got venison (dark, lean), celery root purée (pale, smooth), sunchokes (golden, textured). What else are you thinking for colour or contrast? Right now the plate is very earth-toned.

that's exactly what's bothering me. It's all beige and brown. Beautiful flavours but visually it needs something. I was thinking pickled red cabbage for acidity and colour — just a few shreds — or maybe a herb oil. Parsley oil would give me that green pop

Both are good options and they serve different purposes:

- **Pickled red cabbage** gives you colour (that vibrant purple-magenta), acidity to cut through the richness of the jus and the butter, and textural contrast with its crunch. It lifts the whole plate. A few thin shreds placed deliberately, not a pile.

- **Parsley oil** gives you colour (bright green) and freshness, but it's more subtle in terms of flavour contrast. It's beautiful on a plate but it won't do the same work as the pickle in terms of balancing richness.

My instinct: use both. A small dot or swoosh of parsley oil for the green, and three or four shreds of pickled red cabbage for the acid and the purple. Together they break up the earth tones and give the plate visual depth without cluttering it.

Or -- if you want to stay more classic -- a single sprig of micro herbs. Micro celery would echo the purée and add a tiny pop of green.

both. I like that. The parsley oil is easy — I can blitz it and strain it, keeps for days. The pickled cabbage I'll do in-house with red wine vinegar and a little sugar to balance. Not too sweet though, this isn't a barbecue plate. Just enough acid to make the palate want another bite of venison

Exactly -- the pickle should function as a palate reset, not a condiment. A quick pickle with red wine vinegar, a pinch of sugar, maybe a few juniper berries in the pickling liquid to tie it back to the jus. That creates a flavour thread through the whole dish without being obvious.

So the final plate: venison loin sliced and fanned, celery root purée as the base, roasted sunchoke halves placed cut-side up to show the caramelization, the red wine and juniper jus spooned around, a few shreds of pickled red cabbage, and dots of parsley oil. That's a complete dish -- earthy, gamey, rich, with acid and freshness to balance.

juniper in the pickle. That's a nice touch — ties the whole thing together. You know what, I might also try a second game dish for the menu. I've been thinking about braised wild boar shoulder with root vegetables. Different preparation, heavier, more rustic. For the colder nights when people want something that sticks to them

A braised wild boar shoulder would be a great complement to the venison -- it gives you range on the menu. The venison is refined and precise, the boar is hearty and forgiving. Two different approaches to game that serve different moods.

For the boar, the shoulder is perfect because it's tough and fatty, which means it braises beautifully. Red wine, stock, aromatics, maybe some dried porcini in the braising liquid for that umami depth. Root vegetables can cook right in the braise -- carrots, parsnips, turnips, they'll absorb all that flavour.

What's your source for wild boar? Getting consistent quality can be tricky.

there's a farm in the Laurentians that supplies us — good quality, reliable. The boar is always well-handled. And yes, porcini in the braising liquid, that's exactly what I was thinking. Maybe finish with gremolata for freshness, because braised boar can get very heavy very fast

Gremolata is a smart choice -- the lemon zest and parsley cut through the richness without competing with the deep flavours of the braise. You could do a variation with orange zest instead of lemon for something slightly warmer and more winter-appropriate. Orange and wild boar is a classic combination.

Between the venison loin and the braised boar, you'd have a winter game menu that covers both ends of the spectrum. One elegant, one rustic. Diners who want finesse get the venison. Diners who want comfort get the boar.

orange zest gremolata. Oui. That's better actually, you're right — warmer, more seasonal. D'accord, I'm going to test the venison dish tomorrow and work on the boar this weekend. If both work it goes on the menu next week. Tabarnac, I love winter menus. Game season is the best part of this job

Game season is when chefs get to play with the most interesting ingredients. You've clearly got a vision for this menu. Test the venison first -- the chocolate in the jus is the wild card, so start with a very small amount and taste as you go. You can always add more but you can't take it out. And make sure the celery root purée is seasoned aggressively enough to stand up to the venison. Root vegetable purées can go flat if they're under-seasoned.

Good luck with both tests. Winter on a plate -- that's the goal.
